documents.content: Google interviews lean heavily on data structures and algorithms — more than Amazon, more than Meta. Expect 4-5 coding rounds in the loop, each 45 minutes. The questions trend toward LeetCode medium-hard with at least one hard. Topics that come up often: graphs (especially weighted graph traversals, MST, topological sort), dynamic programming (Google loves DP), trees (LCA, serialization, traversals), string problems (suffix arrays, edit distance variants), and design problems that require choosing the right data structure. Pure brute-force solutions that pass test cases aren't enough; Google interviewers will push you to optimize and analyze tradeoffs carefully.

Google's 'Googliness' assessment is real but understated. Interviewers evaluate whether you're collaborative, intellectually curious, and good to work with. Red flags they look for: defensiveness when challenged, refusal to consider alternative approaches, arrogance about past achievements, dismissiveness of the interviewer's hints, and an inability to admit when you're stuck. Green flags: genuine excitement about the problem, willingness to brainstorm openly, asking the interviewer questions about how the team works, and acknowledging when you'd want to research something rather than bluffing an answer. Googliness questions often hide inside technical rounds, not as a separate behavioral interview.

Google L4 (entry SWE) vs L5 (senior) loop differences: L4 loops are 4 rounds — 3 coding + 1 'Googliness and Leadership'. The coding bar is high but bounded — solving the problem with optimal time complexity and clean code is enough. L5 loops add a system design round, raise the coding bar (expect to optimize and discuss space/time tradeoffs), and the Googliness round is replaced by a hiring manager round that probes leadership and influence. After loops, your packet goes to the hiring committee — a group of senior Googlers who never met you and decide based on the written feedback. This is why your interviewers' written feedback matters more than their verbal impression.

Google system design problems lean academic and infrastructure-flavored: design a distributed key-value store, design a distributed file system, design YouTube's video processing pipeline, design Google Docs collaborative editing, design a global CDN, design Google's web crawler. Less common: design Twitter, design Uber (these are more typical at companies that ship consumer apps). Be prepared to discuss consensus protocols (Paxos, Raft) at L5+, content-addressable storage, log-structured merge trees, and how distributed systems handle network partitions. Reading the Spanner paper, the GFS paper, and the MapReduce paper isn't strictly required but helps with vocabulary.
